# Technical Specification

<!-- Generated by Arnold from tokenburner-3000-concept-v2.md. Edit directly or run /arnold:decide to update. -->

## Stack

| Layer | Choice | Rationale | Source |
|-------|--------|-----------|--------|
| Language | JavaScript/TypeScript | Existing prototype is React | (code-derived) |
| UI Framework | React | Prototype built in React with component architecture | (code-derived) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS | Prototype uses Tailwind utility classes throughout | (code-derived) |
| Fonts | Chakra Petch + Space Mono | CRT/retro aesthetic, monospace for numbers/stats | (code-derived) |
| AI — Anthropic | Claude API | Fighter + potential referee | (spec-stated) |
| AI — OpenAI | OpenAI API (GPT-4o, etc.) | Fighter | (spec-stated) |
| AI — Google | Google AI API (Gemini) | Fighter | (spec-stated) |
| AI — Open Models | Groq / Together / Fireworks (Llama, Mistral) | Fast + cheap fighters | (spec-stated) |
| AI — Commentator | Grok (xAI) or similar sassy model | Sassy personality fits the commentator role | (user-stated) |
| AI — Referee | Ironic/funny model (TBD — see decisions) | Needs good judgment + comedic timing | (user-stated) |
| Storage (V1) | Local Storage | Quickest to deploy, modular, no backend dependency | (user-stated) |
| Storage (Future) | TBD — needs accounts/auth for online play | Future requirement, not V1 | (user-stated) |

## Architecture

The app follows a client-side-first architecture for V1. The frontend handles fighter selection, arena selection, and orchestrates parallel API calls to AI providers. Both model responses stream in real-time to a split-screen view. When the fight ends (timer or both done), a referee model receives both outputs and returns a structured JSON verdict.

Fight results, leaderboard data, and user history are stored in local storage for V1. This keeps deployment simple (static site + API keys) while remaining modular enough to swap in a backend database later when accounts and online play are added.

The existing prototype (`docs/tokenburner3000.html`) demonstrates the full game loop with mock data: landing page, fighter select, arena select, fight streaming, and verdict display. The production app will replace mock generators with real API streaming.

## Visual Design

- **Palette:** Neon magenta (#ff00ff), cyan (#00ffff), green (#39ff14), orange (#ff5e00), red (#ff003c) on near-black (#050505)
- **Effects:** CRT scanlines overlay, flicker animations, hue-rotate on title, matrix dot grid background, typewriter cursor on streaming text
- **Typography:** Chakra Petch for headings/UI, Space Mono for numbers/stats/receipts
- **Aesthetic:** UFC broadcast meets crypto dashboard on fire. Neon box shadows, dashed-border receipts, ping animations for live indicators

## Constraints

- Both model API calls must run simultaneously, not sequentially (spec-stated)
- Responses must stream in real-time with visible token counting (spec-stated)
- 60-second fight timer; if a model finishes early, its response stays while opponent continues (spec-stated)
- Token counting should use appropriate tokenizers per provider for accuracy (spec-stated)
- Cost calculation uses published per-token pricing for each model (spec-stated)
- The site never breaks character — dead-serious combat sports voice at all times (spec-stated)

## Open Technical Questions

- Exact referee model selection — see `docs/decisions/006-referee-model.md`
- How to handle API key management in a client-side app (user's keys vs hosted proxy?)
- Token counting accuracy: use provider-specific tokenizers or estimate from stream chunks?
- Rate limiting strategy when multiple users fight simultaneously (future, post-V1)
